card.title: The Osborne Effect
card.teaser: How a CEO killed his own company by announcing a product too early — and gave the phenomenon a name.
card.coverImagePrompt: A dusty 1980s beige computer on a dealership showroom desk, empty chairs in the background, a clipboard with falling sales charts, melancholic amber lighting

lessons.title: The 24-pound hit
lessons.body: In 1981, Adam Osborne released the world's first mass-market portable computer. It weighed 24 pounds, looked like a sewing machine, and sold 10,000 units a month. By 1985, Osborne Computer Corporation was bankrupt. The story of how it fell became a cautionary tale every startup founder still quotes today.

visual.imagePrompt: The iconic 1981 Osborne 1 portable computer on a wooden desk, closed-box briefcase form, CRT and chiclet keyboard visible, nostalgic lighting

lessons.title: What the 'Osborne effect' means
lessons.body: The term describes what happens when a company pre-announces a future product so enthusiastically that customers stop buying the current one. Sales of the old model collapse — but the new model isn't ready to ship. The company bleeds cash in the gap, and sometimes doesn't survive it.

keyTerms.definition: When premature announcement of a next product kills demand for the current product
Publicly revealing a future product before it's ready for sale

visual.imagePrompt: A sharp downward sales graph on a CRT monitor, red glowing numbers, 1980s office at dusk

lessons.title: The actual story is messier
lessons.body: Osborne is blamed for bragging about the upcoming 'Executive' model in early 1983, freezing customers in place. But the truth involves bigger wounds: IBM had entered the market, the company had ballooned its expenses, and compatibility with the IBM PC was the real standard customers wanted. Preannouncement was a contributing cause, not the whole one.

visual.imagePrompt: A detective-style desk covered in old financial reports, a magnifying glass hovering over a 1983 memo, warm lamp light

lessons.title: The effect keeps reappearing
lessons.body: Sega's early pivot to the Saturn cratered Genesis sales. Nokia's Symbian-to-Windows-Phone pivot in 2011 was dubbed the 'Elop effect' after CEO Stephen Elop's 'burning platform' memo — devices collapsed in anticipation of a product that wouldn't ship for months. Netflix's Qwikster reveal, Google's Stadia teasers, countless phone makers: the pattern is older than any one company.
lessons.funFact: Osborne Computer's revenue dropped from over $100 million in 1982 to near zero by the end of 1983 — a faster collapse than most recessions.

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized 'burning platform' — an office floor on fire as executives stare at phones, editorial magazine illustration style

lessons.title: Timing is a weapon that cuts both ways
lessons.body: Hype is oxygen for startups — but oxygen too early can suffocate the thing already keeping the lights on. The Osborne effect is really a rule about cash flow and trust: don't tell customers the future until you can sell it to them. Apple's 'one more thing' exists for exactly this reason.

quiz.question: What does the 'Osborne effect' describe?
options: A marketing campaign that goes viral
When preannouncing a future product kills demand for the current one
A pricing strategy based on rarity
A legal term for false advertising
quiz.explanation: It describes a collapse in current sales because customers wait for a promised future product that isn't shipping yet.

quiz.question: According to historians, what else contributed to Osborne's bankruptcy?
options: IBM entering the market and bloated company expenses
quiz.explanation: IBM's PC set a new standard, and Osborne had scaled up costs aggressively — both were major factors besides the preannouncement.

quiz.question: Which modern example is often compared to the Osborne effect?
quiz.explanation: Nokia's CEO publicly declared the Symbian platform doomed months before Windows Phone shipped — Symbian sales cratered in the interim.
